normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  (∃ t : Tree, t = tree) → True
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit precondition is asserted in the code, but the module comment states: both keys are present in the tree. We will capture this as a separate correctness obligation below.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
let lo : Int := if p ≤ q then p else q
let hi : Int := if p ≤ q then q else p
in lo ≤ hi ∧ (lo = min p q) ∧ (hi = max p q)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation normalizes the inputs so that lo = min(p,q) and hi = max(p,q), ensuring lo ≤ hi.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree),
  BST tree →
  ∀ (x : Int),
    x ∈ keys tree →
    (match tree with
     | Nil => False
     | Node l k r =>
         (x = k ∨ (x < k ∧ x ∈ keys l) ∨ (x > k ∧ x ∈ keys r)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: To justify the branch decisions (go left if hi < k, go right if lo > k), we need the tree to satisfy the BST ordering: all keys in the left subtree are < k and all keys in the right subtree are > k.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  let lo := min p q
  let hi := max p q
  in
  ∀ (cur : Tree),
    cur ∈ subtree tree →
    (p ∈ keys cur ∧ q ∈ keys cur) →
    (match cur with
     | Nil => False
     | Node left k right =>
         (if hi < k then (p ∈ keys left ∧ q ∈ keys left)
          else if lo > k then (p ∈ keys right ∧ q ∈ keys right)
          else (lo ≤ k ∧ k ≤ hi)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: At each step, assuming both keys are still in the current subtree, the BST property implies: if hi < k then both keys must be in the left subtree; if lo > k then both keys must be in the right subtree; otherwise k lies between lo and hi.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  let lo := min p q
  let hi := max p q
  in
  ∀ (cur : Tree),
    cur ∈ subtree tree →
    (∃ n : Nat,
      runSteps tree p q n = Some (lca_bst tree p q))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The loop terminates by descending the tree: each iteration moves to a strict subtree (left or right) and eventually reaches Nil or returns Some(k).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int) (r : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some r →
  r ∈ keys tree ∧
  (r isAncestorOf tree p) ∧
  (r isAncestorOf tree q)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: If the function returns Some r, then r is a key in the tree and is an ancestor of both p and q.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  BST tree →
  p ∈ keys tree →
  q ∈ keys tree →
  ∃ r : Int, lca_bst tree p q = some r
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Under the documented assumption that both keys are present in the BST, the search will not fall off the tree and will return Some.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q r : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some r →
  (r isAncestorOf tree p ∧ r isAncestorOf tree q) ∧
  ∀ (s : Int),
    s ≠ r →
    (s isAncestorOf tree p ∧ s isAncestorOf tree q) →
    (depth tree s < depth tree r)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned key r is the lowest (deepest) node that is a common ancestor of p and q.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int) (k : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some k →
  min p q ≤ k ∧ k ≤ max p q
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Because the algorithm returns when lo ≤ k ≤ hi at some node, the returned key must lie between the two query keys.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (p q : Int),
  lca_bst Nil p q = none
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: If the tree is empty, the function returns None.
